title: Botensilimab (Fc-enhanced anti–cytotoxic lymphocyte-association protein-4 antibody) plus Balstilimab (anti–PD-1 antibody) in patients with relapsed/refractory metastatic sarcomas
abstract:
Purpose: Outcomes for patients with advanced sarcomas are poor and there is a high unmet need to develop novel therapies. The purpose of this phase I study was to define the safety and efficacy of botensilimab (BOT), an Fc-enhanced anti–cytotoxic lymphocyte-association protein-4 antibody, plus balstilimab (BAL), an anti–PD-1 antibody, in advanced sarcomas.

Methods: BOT was administered intravenously (IV) at 1 mg/kg or 2 mg/kg once every 6 weeks in combination with BAL IV at 3 mg/kg once every 2 weeks for up to 2 years. The primary end point was to determine dose-limiting toxicities during the dose-escalation period. Secondary end points include objective response rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), disease control rate, and progression-free survival (PFS) by RECIST 1.1. Exploratory end points include assessing patient biomarkers including tumor mutational burden, cytokines, and PD-L1 expression.

Results: Overall, 64 patients with sarcoma were treated; all were evaluable for safety and 52 for efficacy. The most common treatment-related adverse event (TRAE) was diarrhea/colitis occurring in 35.9% of patients, with grade 3 in 6.3% of patients. No grade 4 or 5 TRAEs were reported. For all evaluable patients, ORR was 19.2% (95% CI, 9.6 to 32.5), and 27.8% (95% CI, 9.7 to 53.5) for evaluable patients with angiosarcoma (n = 18); 33.3% in visceral and 22.2% in cutaneous subtypes. Median PFS for evaluable patients was 4.4 months (95% CI, 2.8 to 6.1), with a 6-month PFS rate of 36% (95% CI, 22 to 50) and a median DOR of 21.7 months (95% CI, 1.9 to not reached).

Conclusion: The combination of BOT/BAL demonstrated promising efficacy and safety in a large cohort of heavily pretreated sarcoma patients. This encouraging activity warrants further investigation (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT03860272 ).

ABSTRACT
PURPOSE
Outcomes for patients with advanced sarcomas are poor and there is a high
unmet need to develop novel therapies. The purpose of this phase I study was to
deﬁne the safety and efﬁcacy of botensilimab (BOT), an Fc-enhanced anti–
cytotoxic lymphocyte-association protein-4 antibody, plus balstilimab (BAL),
an anti–PD-1 antibody, in advanced sarcomas.
METHODS
BOT was administered intravenously (IV) at 1 mg/kg or 2 mg/kg once every
6 weeks in combination with BAL IV at 3 mg/kg once every 2 weeks for up to
2 years. The primary end point was to determine dose-limiting toxicities during
the dose-escalation period. Secondary end points include objective response
rate (ORR), duration of response (DOR), disease control rate, and progression-
free survival (PFS) by RECIST 1.1. Exploratory end points include assessing
patient biomarkers including tumor mutational burden, cytokines, and PD-L1
expression.
RESULTS
Overall, 64 patients with sarcoma were treated; all were evaluable for safety and
52 for efﬁcacy. The most common treatment-related adverse event (TRAE) was
diarrhea/colitis occurring in 35.9% of patients, with grade 3 in 6.3% of patients.
No grade 4 or 5 TRAEs were reported. For all evaluable patients, ORR was 19.2%
(95% CI, 9.6 to 32.5), and 27.8% (95% CI, 9.7 to 53.5) for evaluable patients with
angiosarcoma (n 5 18); 33.3% in visceral and 22.2% in cutaneous subtypes.
Median PFS for evaluable patients was 4.4 months (95% CI, 2.8 to 6.1), with
a 6-month PFS rate of 36% (95% CI, 22 to 50) and a median DOR of 21.7 months
(95% CI, 1.9 to not reached).
CONCLUSION
The combination of BOT/BAL demonstrated promising efﬁcacy and safety in a
large cohort of heavily pretreated sarcoma patients. This encouraging activity
warrants further investigation (ClinicalTrials.gov identiﬁer: NCT03860272).
INTRODUCTION
Sarcomas are heterogeneous neoplasms, with more than
150 different subtypes of bone and soft tissue tumors that
collectively make up approximately 1% of all adult cancers.1
In the metastatic setting, overall survival (OS) with
treatment remains poor, with 5-year survival rates ap-
proximately 16%, thus novel treatments are urgently
needed, including immunotherapies that have the po-
tential for durable responses but are not approved for most
subtypes.2 Common adult sarcomas including leiomyo-
sarcoma (LMS) and dedifferentiated liposarcoma (dLPS)
have response rates <10% to PD-1 monotherapy,3,4 likely
because of poor tumor immunogenicity, with low tumor
mutational burden (TMB), suppressive microenviron-
ments, dysregulated antigen presentation machinery, and
low expression of immune-related genes.5 An exception is
cutaneous angiosarcoma (cAS) arising in the face and
scalp, which exhibits an ultraviolet (UV) genetic signature
akin to melanoma.6 In a small study of ipilimumab and
nivolumab, tumor responses were noted in four of eight
patients with cAS, including three of ﬁve patients with
face/scalp disease.7 However, only one of seven patients
responded with noncutaneous or visceral angiosarcomas

are considered immunologically cold.
Botensilimab (BOT), a multifunctional anti–cytotoxic
lymphocyte-association protein (CTLA-4) antibody, retains
an Fc-enhanced IgG1-region producing superior binding to
activating Fc-gamma receptors (FcGRs) compared with
conventional anti–CTLA-4 antibodies such as ipilimumab.8
This promotes superior engagement of T cells with antigen-
presenting cells (APCs) and natural killer cells, resulting in
enhanced T-cell priming, activation, and memory formation.8
Balstilimab (BAL) is a fully humanized anti–PD-1 antibody
with activity comparable with approved PD-1 inhibitors.9,10
Reported results from a phase I, open-label study of BOT 6
BAL (ClinicalTrials.gov identiﬁer: NCT03860272) have shown
remarkable outcomes in a variety of immune-cold tumors
unlikely to respond to traditional PD-1/CTLA-4 inhibitors,
such as microsatellite stable metastatic colorectal cancer
(MSS mCRC), and tumors refractory to previous checkpoint
blockade.11,12
Here, we report promising safety and efﬁcacy results of BOT/
BAL for patients with advanced/metastatic sarcomas treated
in a large phase I trial. This combination has the potential to
become a new treatment for this group of largely immu-
nologically cold sarcomas, requiring conﬁrmatory studies.
METHODS
Study Design and Participants
C-800-01 is an open-label, phase Ib, multicenter study eval-
uating the safety, tolerability, efﬁcacy, and pharmacokinetic/
pharmacodynamic proﬁles of BOT 6 BAL (ClinicalTrials.gov
identiﬁer:
NCT03860272,
see
Protocol,
Amendment
8).
Patients were enrolled and treated between October 1, 2020,
and January 24, 2024, at 12 sites across the United States and
the United Kingdom. Patients with sarcoma were enrolled to
the dose-escalation portion of the study, which was a 3 1 3
design, and within a later expansion cohort. The full study
design, results of dose escalation, and efﬁcacy and safety
results for the largest tumor cohort (MSS mCRC) have been
recently reported.12
Eligible patients were age 18 years and older with measurable
disease per RECIST version 1.1 and had an Eastern Cooper-
ative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status (PS) of 0
or 1 with adequate end-organ function. Previous immune
checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) were permitted. Patients must
have had a conﬁrmed diagnosis of refractory sarcoma for
which no standard therapy was available or where standard
therapies previously failed. The study was conducted in
compliance with the Declaration of Helsinki and Interna-
tional Conference on Harmonisation Guidelines for Good
Clinical Practice and was approved by the institutional re-
view board at each institution. Patients provided informed
written consent.
Procedures
During dose escalation, BOT was administered intravenously
(IV) once every 3 weeks or once every 6 weeks at dose levels
of 0.1-3 mg/kg for up to 2 years. BAL was administered IV
once every 2 weeks (up to 2 years) at a dose level of 3 mg/kg
when combined with BOT. Patients enrolled on the dose-
expansion portion of the study received 1 or 2 mg/kg of BOT
(once every 6 weeks) up to 2 years in combination with BAL
3 mg/kg (once every 2 weeks) up to 2 years. Patients were
permitted to crossover from BOT monotherapy to the
combination including treatment beyond progression.
CONTEXT
Key Objective
To deﬁne the safety and efﬁcacy of botensilimab (BOT) plus balstilimab (BAL) in advanced sarcomas.
Knowledge Generated
Treatment with an Fc-enhanced anti–cytotoxic lymphocyte-association protein-4 antibody BOT plus BAL (anti–PD-1)
induced durable responses across immunologically cold soft tissue sarcoma types, including visceral angiosarcoma and
leiomyosarcoma, historically unlikely to respond to traditional checkpoint blockade. Diarrhea/colitis was the most common
immune-mediated treatment-related adverse event, manageable with the use of steroids and anti–tumor necrosis factor
alpha therapy.
Relevance (R.G. Maki)
There are no regulator-approved immune checkpoint inhibitors for patients with metastatic sarcoma beyond alveolar soft
part sarcoma in the United States. This study provides a potential route forward for use of immunotherapy in soft tissue
sarcomas, most of which have low tumor mutational burden and are PD-L1–negative.*
*Relevance section written by JCO Associate Editor Robert G. Maki, MD, PhD, FACP, FASCO.

End Points
The primary end point was safety and toxicity, by deter-
mining the occurrence of dose-limiting toxicities (DLTs)
during dose escalation as previously reported. Assessment of
severity and duration of treatment-emergent adverse events
(TEAEs) and laboratory abnormalities, according to the
National Cancer Institute’s Common Terminology Criteria
for Adverse Events (NCI-CTCAE version 5.0) was a secondary
end point.
Additional secondary end points included (1) objective
response rate (ORR); (2) duration of response (DOR); (3)
disease control rate (DCR), deﬁned as a complete response
(CR) or partial response (PR) or stable disease (SD) of at
least 6 weeks; and (4) progression-free survival (PFS), all
per RECIST 1.1 on the basis of investigator assessment. The
clinical beneﬁt rate (CBR) was also reported and was de-
ﬁned as a best response of CR, PR, or SD for ≥24 weeks.
Imaging was performed once every 6 weeks (63 days)
from the ﬁrst treatment. To better describe patterns of
response
with
immunotherapy
including
pseudoprog-
ression, responses by modiﬁed RECIST 1.1 for immune-
based therapeutics (iRECIST; speciﬁcally designed for
assessing response to immunologic agents v chemother-
apeutics) on the basis of investigator assessment were also
collected as part of an exploratory analysis.
Statistical Analysis
The data cutoff date was July 25, 2024, and the safety
analysis population includes all patients who received at
least one dose of study drug (n 5 64). Safety end points were
tabulated by dose level, using descriptive statistics, and the
severity of adverse events was graded using NCI-CTCAE
whenever possible. The efﬁcacy evaluable set includes all
treated patients with at least one postbaseline 6-week
tumor imaging scan (n 5 52). Descriptive statistics were
used in general to summarize trial results, including means,
medians, ranges, and measures of variability. Qualitative
variables were summarized by counts and percentages. Best
overall response was deﬁned as a conﬁrmed CR or PR. For
patients with a CR or PR, DOR was summarized using
Kaplan-Meier methods, as were PFS and OS for all patients.
Median time to event(s) and 95% CI for the median were
provided.
Correlative Studies
TMB and FcGR Genotyping
TMB was determined through local clinical testing at
designated reference laboratories. All TMB assessments
were
conducted
using
validated
next-generation
se-
quencing panels, with results reported as mutations/
megabase (mut/Mb). FcGR genotyping was performed as
described.8 Genomic DNA was isolated from blood using
QIAamp Midi Kit and subjected to TaqMan Genotyping to
detect single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) rs396991
(FCGR3A). Data acquisition was performed using ViiA7 or
QuantStudio instruments.
Cytokine Analysis
Pretreatment plasma was prepared from blood collected in
NaHep tubes and processed immediately at clinical sites.
Plasma aliquots were stored at –80°C. Interleukin-6 (IL-6)
levels in plasma were measured using the Meso Scale Dis-
covery V-PLEX Plus Proinﬂammatory Panel 1 electro-
chemiluminescence assay (K15049G; Precision for Medicine,
Frederick, MD).
Tumor PD-L1 Immunohistochemistry
PD-L1 protein expression was evaluated in pretreatment
biopsy
specimens
using
immunohistochemistry
28-8
pharmDx qualitative immunohistochemical assay (Dako/
Agilent, Cat#SK005) and examined by a pathologist. Tissue
samples with <100 tumor cells/slide were considered
nonevaluable. PD-L1 expression in all evaluable samples
was assessed by combined proportional score (CPS) as
described.8
RESULTS
Dose Escalation and Dose Expansion
During the dose-escalation portion of the study (April 1,
2019-August 31, 2023; data cut-off, July 25, 2024), 83 pa-
tients were treated with BOT monotherapy or combination
BOT/BAL, including six patients with sarcoma. Four received
BOT monotherapy, including two who initially received BOT
monotherapy IV at 2 mg/kg once every 3 weeks and crossed
over to the combination of BOT IV at 2 mg/kg once every
6 weeks plus BAL IV at 3 mg/kg once every 2 weeks; and two
received the 1 or 2 mg/kg BOT/BAL combination (BOT IV at 1
or 2 mg/kg once every 6 weeks and BAL IV at 3 mg/kg once
every 2 weeks). No DLTs occurred during the dose-escalation
period and a maximum tolerated dose was not reached
(NR).12 The recommended phase II dose is expected to be
either the 1 or 2 mg/kg dose of BOT for sarcoma.
During dose expansion, 63 patients with sarcoma were
treated, with 60 receiving 1 or 2 mg/kg BOT plus BAL (BOT IV
at 1 or 2 mg/kg once every 6 weeks and BAL IV at 3 mg/kg
once every 2 weeks), and three who received a ﬁxed dose of
BOT IV at 150 mg once every 6 weeks and BAL IV at 450 mg
once every 3 weeks, but who were not included in the
analysis. Overall, 64 patients were treated and evaluable for
safety and 52 patients had at least one postbaseline 6-week
imaging scan and were efﬁcacy-evaluable (Fig 1).
Patients

were consistent with general trends among patients with

Safety
Safety is reported for all 64 patients who received at least one
dose of treatment (Table 2; Appendix Table A1, online only).
Forty-seven patients received BOT IV at 1 mg/kg once every
6 weeks plus BAL IV at 3 mg/kg once every 2 weeks, and
15 received 2 mg/kg BOT IV once every 6 weeks plus BAL IV at
3 mg/kg once every 2 weeks. Two patients received BOT
monotherapy IV at 2 mg/kg once every 3 weeks and crossed
over to 2 mg/kg BOT/BAL (BOT IV at 2 mg/kg once every 6
weeks plus BAL IV at 3 mg/kg once every 2 weeks). All pa-
tients experienced at least one TEAE (62.5% grade 3;
Appendix Table A1) and 82.8% experienced at least one
treatment-related adverse event (TRAE; 17.2% grade 3;
Table 2). No grade 4 or 5 TRAEs were reported. The most
common TRAE was diarrhea/colitis occurring in 35.9% of
patients (grade 3, 6.3%). Incidence was similar among pa-
tients receiving 1 or 2 mg/kg of BOT. Other common TRAEs
occurring in more than 10.0% of patients included fatigue
(any grade 26.6%, 12.5% grade 3), pyrexia (any grade 21.9%,
no grade 3), chills (any grade 17.2%, no grade 3), rash (any
grade 17.2%, 1.6% grade 3), rash maculopapular (any grade
14.1%, no grade 3), myalgia (any grade 10.9%, 1.6% grade 3),
and hypothyroidism (any grade 10.9%; no grade 3). There
were no TRAEs of pneumonitis, myocarditis, or hypophysitis
reported in this cohort.
Approximately 51.6% of patients experienced at least one
immune-mediated TRAE, deﬁned as related to treatment
and subsequently treated with steroids (of any dose) or other

immunosuppressants. Tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-a)
inhibitors (such as inﬂiximab) were permitted in steroid-
sensitive settings to manage immune-mediated diarrhea/
colitis, even in the nonrefractory low-grade setting.13,14 Oral
or intravenous steroids and intravenous TNF-alpha inhib-
itors were prescribed per investigator’s discretion. Steroids
were typically prescribed at 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (including
once, once daily, twice daily, 3 times/day, 4 times/day, every
6 hours, at bedtime, or as needed), while TNF-alpha in-
hibitors were typically prescribed at 5-10 mg/kg IV once.
Patients who received TNF-a inhibitors were permitted to
continue study drug if toxicity was resolved and grade ≤2
(Appendix Fig A1). If a patient had a grade 3 event related to
BOT but unrelated to BAL, they were allowed to continue
BAL. In total, there were 38 events of immune-mediated
diarrhea/colitis, composed of the preferred terms of
diarrhea, colitis, immune-mediated enteritis, autoim-
mune colitis, enterocolitis, and GI inﬂammation (Ap-
pendix Table A2), 37 (97.4%) of which were treated with
immunosuppressants (TNF-a inhibitors and/or cortico-
steroids), and 33 (89.2%) of those resolved. Other com-
mon immune-mediated TRAEs included skin reactions,
occurring in 17.2% of patients, and hypothyroidism in 10.9%.
Nondiarrhea/colitis immune-mediated TRAEs were managed
according to investigator’s discretion.15
Efﬁcacy
Patients evaluable for efﬁcacy had a baseline and at least
one postbaseline 6-week tumor imaging assessment for
response (n 5 52) with RECIST-conﬁrmed investigator-
assessed responses reported (Table 3). Overall, 10 of 52
evaluable patients achieved a response by RECIST 1.1 (ORR,
19.2% [95% CI, 9.6 to 32.5]). Responses were observed in
patients with cAS, vAS, dLPS, soft tissue LMS, and UPS
(Fig 1A, waterfall plot). There were no conﬁrmed CRs, al-
though there was one unconﬁrmed CR in a patient with cAS
(PR by RECIST). Importantly, responses were durable
(Fig 1B, swimmer plot) with a median DOR of 21.7 months
(95% CI, 1.9 to NR). Twenty-four additional patients
achieved SD as best response, for a DCR of 65.4% (95% CI,
50.9 to 78.0). The CBR was 32.7% (95% CI, 20.3 to 47.1),
and median PFS was 4.4 months (95% CI, 2.8 to 6.1), with a
6-month PFS rate of 36% (95% CI, 21.9 to 50.2; Fig 2A).
With a median follow-up of 9.1 months (range, 1.4-39.7),
median OS was NR (95% CI, 14.0 to NR), and the 12-month
OS rate measured 69% (95% CI, 52.3 to 80.8; Fig 2B).
Outside of RECIST, additional patients achieved clinical
beneﬁt with BOT/BAL including a patient with vAS who had
pseudoprogression at 6 weeks (PD by RECIST) followed by a
deep PR in target lesions, which persists off therapy to date
(2 years). When assessed by iRECIST, the conﬁrmed ORR
was 21.2% in evaluable patients (95% CI, 11.1 to 34.7; Ap-
pendix Table A3).
Given the relatively large number of patients enrolled and
evaluable for efﬁcacy with AS (n 5 18), we performed an
exploratory analysis of efﬁcacy in this subtype (Appendix
Table A4 and Fig A2; Fig 3). The RECIST-conﬁrmed ORR for
AS was 27.8%, and 33.3% by iRECIST, with a DCR of 77.8% by
RECIST 1.1, and 83.3% by iRECIST (Appendix Table A5). The
CBR was 38.9% by RECIST 1.1 and 44.4% by iRECIST.
The difference between RECIST 1.1 and iRECIST is related to
the previously mentioned vAS patient with pseudoprog-
ression (Appendix Fig A3). Importantly, RECIST-conﬁrmed
ORRs in patients with cAS and vAS were 22.2% and 33.3%,
respectively (Appendix Table A6). The median PFS for pa-
tients with AS was 4.7 months (95% CI, 2.8 to NR), with a
6-month PFS rate of 48.4% (95% CI, 21.4 to 71.0), and a
median DOR of 21.7 months (95% CI, 1.9 to NR).
Regarding additional subtypes, the ORR in soft tissue LMS
was 13.3% (two PRs out of 15 patients), and an additional nine
patients had clinical beneﬁt, with a DCR of 73.3% (11/15).

Correlative Studies
To investigate potential biomarkers of response to BOT/BAL,
we examined peripheral IL-6 levels,16 PD-L1 expression,
TMB, and FCGR3A status (Data Supplement, online only).
Lower peripheral IL-6 levels were signiﬁcantly associated
with improved OS (Appendix Fig A4A). Patients were
stratiﬁed on the basis of peripheral IL-6 levels, with a cutoff
at the lower limit of quantiﬁcation (LLoQ) of 0.63 pg/mL.
Patients with IL-6 levels ≤LLoQ showed markedly improved
OS compared with those with IL-6 >LLoQ (P 5 .009), with a
hazard ratio (HR) of 0.15 (95% CI, 0.04 to 0.63).
Despite a trend toward improved survival in patients with
PD-L1 CPS ≥1 compared with those with PD-L1 CPS<1, PD-L1
expression did not signiﬁcantly correlate with OS (Appendix
Fig A4B; P 5 .26; HR, 0.51 [95% CI, 0.15 to 1.65]). High TMB
and FcgRIIIA (CD16)-V158F SNPs have been shown to be
predictive markers of clinical response to ipilimumab in
patients with advanced melanomas.17 However, in our co-
hort, patients with V/F or V/V genotypes had similar survival
outcomes to those with the F/F genotype (P 5 .45; HR, 0.81
[95% CI, 0.24 to 1.94]; Appendix Fig A4C), consistent with
the ability of BOT to bind strongly to high- and low-afﬁnity
CD16 receptors.8 Notably, most patients’ tumors in our study
exhibited low TMB (<10 mut/Mb), consistent with the low
TMB observed among sarcomas compared with other cancer
types, which may partially explain low response rates of
sarcoma to conventional ICIs.18,19
DISCUSSION
Most sarcomas are increasingly recognized as immuno-
logically cold tumors. Apart from a few subtypes, tran-
scriptomic data suggest that up to 80% of the most
common subtypes of adult sarcomas have low TMB, de-
fective antigen presentation, and low expression of innate
and adaptive immune signatures.5 The two largest studies
of combination CTLA-4/PD-1–directed therapies reported
all-comer
response
rates
of
16%
for
ipilimumab/
nivolumab,20 and 12% by immune-related RECIST (irRE-
CIST) with tremelimumab/durvalumab.21 Patients with AS
have derived greater beneﬁt from ICIs, particularly cuta-
neous head and neck tumors with a UV signature that re-
sembles melanoma.6 Angiosarcomas arising in visceral
locations or primary breast sites more often exhibit mu-
tations in KDR, TP53, and PIK3CA, which may explain de-
creased responses to ICIs.22

Antibodies targeted against CTLA-4 are thought to help
improve engagement within the APC and T-cell synapse,
helping to generate and expand tumor-speciﬁc T-cell
priming, activation, and clonality, to be augmented by
downstream PD-1 blockade. This is desirable in poorly im-
munogenic tumors such as sarcomas, and the response rate
of the combination of ipilimumab/nivolumab performed
better historically than nivolumab monotherapy.20 Preclin-
ical modeling and correlative analysis suggests BOT extends
the reach of CTLA-4 blockade, with superior depletion of Treg
cells, expansion of activated phenotypes of myeloid cells,
along with potentiation of T-cell activation.8 Trial data
suggests that BOT appears to improve efﬁcacy of ICIs in
immune-cold
tumors,
including
MSS
mCRC
and
ICI-

melanoma
and

lung

among others.

had a RECIST-conﬁrmed ORR of 27.8%. The ORRs for cAS

These outcomes compare favorably to previous experience of

perior activity in immune-cold sarcomas.
The overall safety proﬁle was manageable and as expected
for the class of ICIs. As with other tumor types included in the
phase I trial, the most common treatment-related toxicity
associated with BOT was diarrhea/colitis; however, most
episodes could be managed successfully with early inter-
vention including TNF-a inhibitors and rapid steroid ta-
pering minimizing time without treatment. Importantly,
this strategy represents an adapted paradigm for manage-
ment
of
ICI-associated
diarrhea/colitis,
which
should
improve safety proﬁles of other CTLA-4 inhibitors. The
absence of other immune toxicities, such as myocarditis,
pneumonitis, and hypophysitis, may be explained by the
decreased
complement
binding
resulting
from
Fc-
engineering of BOT.
Our hypothesis-generating correlative data revealed com-
plex relationships between biomarkers and treatment out-
comes with BOT/BAL in sarcoma. We observed a trend
toward improved survival in patients with PD-L1 CPS ≥1%
versus CPS <1%. Although PD-L1 has not been a conclusive
biomarker for response to PD-1–directed therapy in
sarcomas, this potential association with response to CTLA-
4 blockade warrants further study.23,24 As expected, patients
with sarcoma had low TMB (<10 mut/Mb), further high-
lighting the potential of BOT/BAL to elicit responses in

affect response to ipilimumab in melanoma, did not sig-

Finally, lower peripheral IL-6 levels were signiﬁcantly as-
sociated with improved OS, consistent with observations in
other cancers.16
An emerging but controversial biomarker being explored in
sarcomas is the presence of tertiary lymphoid structures
(TLS), with TLS presence associated with pembrolizumab
efﬁcacy in advanced soft tissue sarcomas.5,25-27 Although
our study did not assess TLS, our previous data describing
mechanisms of action of BOT in patients with advanced
solid cancers suggest that enhanced Fc-effector functions
could promote conditions favorable for TLS formation
through multiple pathways including activation of type-1
conventional dendritic cells, expansion of B and activated
CD41
T-follicular-helper
cells,
and
induction
of
key
chemokines associated with immune recruitment to the
tumor microenvironment.8
Our study has several limitations. First, our efﬁcacy results
are reported for patients who had at least one postbaseline
6-week imaging scan. As is common in phase I studies, there
were 12 patients who enrolled who did not reach protocol-
deﬁned imaging assessments. Reasons for discontinuation
included rapid progression by RECIST 1.1, disease-related
adverse events leading to signiﬁcant medical complica-
tions, or death. Similarly, PFS and OS curves are heavily
censored because of several patients withdrawing consent
early. Importantly, immunotherapy requires time to exert its
effects.
Overall, we observed promising efﬁcacy and safety in a
relatively large cohort of patients with heavily pretreated and
heterogeneous sarcomas, including exciting activity in vAS
and disease control in LMS. Further exploration of activity
and safety in selected sarcoma subsets, including ASs, is
warranted.

FIG A1. Algorithm for management of immune-mediated diarrhea/colitis. This algorithm reﬂects expert guidance and evolving literature on the
management of immune-mediated colitis. It is intended to be used for patients receiving BOT-based therapy. This algorithm is not intended to
replace the independent medical or professional judgment of physicians or other health care providers in the context of individual clinical
circumstances to determine a patient’s care. aAlthough the CTCAE version 5.0 classiﬁes and grades diarrhea and colitis as separate entities, in the
clinical setting of ICI therapy for cancer, immune-related diarrhea and colitis occur along a common spectrum. Because there is an underlying
common pathology, and inﬂammation often involves both the large and small intestines, immune-related diarrhea and colitis are considered and
discussed jointly.28 bAssess patient as soon as possible with a goal of intervening within 24 hours of the onset of symptoms. The initial as-
sessment may be over the phone or in person, but full workup requires in-person evaluation as soon as possible. Assessment should include
number of stools above baseline, consistency (ie, watery), recent illnesses, infectious exposures, and recent antibiotics. Also assess for potential
alarm symptoms (see 5 below). cCTCAEv5 grading: grade 1: increase of less than four stools per day over baseline; or mild increase in ostomy
output over baseline. Grade 2: increase of four to six stools per day over baseline; or moderate increase in ostomy output over baseline; limiting
instrumental ADLs. Grade 3: increase of ≥seven stools per day over baseline; or severe increase in ostomy output over baseline; limiting self-care
ADLs; hospitalization indicated. Grade 4: life-threatening consequences, urgent intervention indicated. Grade 5: death. dIn a minority of patients
treated with BOT, there is an early-onset, self-limited syndrome that may occur anywhere from hours to 1-2 weeks after infusion. This syndrome
may include any of the following symptoms: fatigue, fever/chills, malaise, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. This syndrome does not represent
immune-related diarrhea/colitis as it is generally self-limited and can be treated symptomatically with nonsteroidal anti-inﬂammatory drugs
(consider 500 mg naproxen twice daily with appropriate GI prophylaxis if tolerated)/acetaminophen/6antimotility agents and supportive
measures. Recommend careful monitoring of any patient who appears to have this syndrome to ensure resolution. The early-onset syndrome may
reoccur with subsequent doses of BOT. However, if it did not occur with the ﬁrst dose, it is unlikely to occur with subsequent doses and alternate
etiologies should be considered, especially immune-related adverse events such as diarrhea/colitis. Timing of symptom onset in relation to BOT
treatment is important. If diarrhea/nausea/vomiting occur >1-2 weeks after dosing, suspicion should be higher for immune-related diarrhea/
colitis/enteritis and lower for the early-onset syndrome, whereas if symptoms develop within <1 week, immune-related diarrhea/colitis requiring
steroids is extremely unlikely. eAny alarm symptom associated with diarrhea/colitis including but not limited to high-grade fever, severe abdominal
pain, and blood in stool (hematochezia or melena) or hemodynamic instability warrant urgent evaluation. Note that fever may be seen with early-
onset syndrome; however, this is a diagnosis of exclusion, especially if symptom onset is >1 week after dosing. fIf patient is unable to present for
in-person evaluation in <24 hours, recommend utilization of oral prednisone that was provided at C1D1 (patient education and prescription) until
they can present to medical facility for workup and treatment. For grade 1-2: prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/d. For grade 3-4: prednisone 1 mg/kg/d.
gRecommend supportive measures immediately: BRAT diet and sufﬁcient oral ﬂuid intake, especially electrolyte-rich ﬂuids. Ensure that any stool
softeners and/or laxatives are discontinued. Antimotility agents are ineffective in the setting of immune-mediated diarrhea/colitis. hRecommend
medical workup for acute-onset diarrhea of infectious or noninfectious etiology, which includes but is not limited to C. diff toxin, stool cultures, and
ova/parasite examination, complete blood count, GI viral panel, complete metabolic panel, amylase/lipase, and inﬂammatory markers per provider
discretion. C. diff is the most common alternate diagnosis and may co-occur with immune-related diarrhea/colitis. i If not already initiated, begin
steroids. For grade 1-2: prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/d or equivalent. For grade 3-4: prednisone 1 mg/kg/d or equivalent, intravenous administration
preferred if this would not result in a delay. jOnce patient has been assessed and nonimmune causes are effectively ruled out, we recommend
treatment with inﬂiximab (5 mg/kg) or equivalent, regardless of response to steroids or grade of diarrhea. Consider 10 mg/kg inﬂiximab or
equivalent for grade 4, or if severe hypoalbuminemia (albumin <2.5) is present. kIf tuberculosis testing (ie, Quantiferon Gold) is positive, then
